journal-meta.journal-id: Am J Respir Cell Mol Biol
Am J Respir Cell Mol Biol

journal-title-group.journal-title: American Journal of Respiratory Cell and Molecular Biology

title-group.article-title: Transcriptomic Profiling of Long COVID-19 in Patients with Interstitial Lung Disease Reveals Dysregulation of Mitochondrial Oxidative Phosphorylation

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2025 by the American Thoracic Society

license.license-p: This article is open access and distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives License 4.0. For commercial usage and reprints, please e-mail Diane Gern.

institution-wrap.institution: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

body.p: The global impact of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) extends beyond acute illness, with long coronavirus disease (long COVID-19; LC) emerging as a persistent, multisystem condition affecting millions of survivors (1). LC is characterized by relapsing, progressive symptoms lasting at least 3 months after infection, accompanied by respiratory manifestations such as interstitial lung disease (ILD) in up to 29% of cases (1, 2). Recent studies implicate mitochondrial gene networks, immune dysregulation, and extracellular matrix remodeling in the pathogenesis of LC-ILD’s most burdensome symptoms: fatigue, cough, and lung fibrosis (3–5). However, the gene regulatory mechanisms of LC-ILD remain poorly understood.

This study dissects the transcriptomic landscape of LC-ILD to better understand the distinct mechanisms of LC-ILD, non-LC–ILD, and healthy states. Patients were enrolled from clinical settings between October 2020 to October 2022. Participants underwent multidisciplinary evaluation of ILD diagnosis, including clinical assessments, pulmonary function tests, and high-resolution computed tomography scans following American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society guidelines (6). Participants with LC-ILD had documented SARS-CoV-2 infection via nasopharyngeal swab or PCR assays and experienced pulmonary symptoms for at least 3 months after infection together with high-resolution computed tomography evidence of ILD. Inclusion criteria for LC-ILD adhered to the 2024 National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine definition (1), emphasizing persistent symptoms including fatigue and cough alongside radiological evidence of ILD. Patients with LC-ILD had absence of prior history of known ILD and underwent systematic exclusion of other causes of ILD during clinical work-up and multidisciplinary evaluation. Patients with non-LC–ILD had no COVID-related symptoms, were SARS-CoV-2 negative at diagnosis, and had never tested positive for the virus. Blood samples were obtained and processed under institutional review board (IRB)-approved protocols (IRB#21-0480; IRB#14163A). RNA sequencing was performed on isolated peripheral blood mononuclear cells.

This study enrolled 36 subjects stratified into three groups: LC-ILD (n = 12), non-LC–ILD (n = 12), and healthy control subjects (n = 12). Although subjects with non-LC–ILD had more frequent cough, radiologic honeycombing, and lung function impairment, the prevalence of fatigue was higher in LC-ILD (Figure 1A). Differentially expressed genes were evaluated in two key comparisons: LC-ILD versus non-LC–ILD and LC-ILD versus non-LC–ILD combined with healthy control subjects from the Genotype-Tissue Expression (GTEx) project (7). The inclusion of healthy control subjects increased the power to detect LC-ILD transcriptional changes; however, because healthy samples were obtained from different time periods, locations, and processing protocols, we performed both comparisons to ensure transcriptional changes are due to biological rather than technical variation (see data supplement for full methods details).

caption.p: (A) Demographic and clinical information for study samples. Groups are age and sex matched. (B) Differentially expressed genes between samples from patients with long coronavirus disease (COVID-19)–interstitial lung disease (LC-ILD) and non-LC–ILD plus healthy control subjects. A positive fold change indicates the gene is upregulated in patients with LC-ILD compared with other samples. The vertical dashed lines mark −1 and 1, and the horizontal dashed line marks P < 10−5. (C) Differentially expressed genes between patients with LC-ILD and non-LC–ILD. Purple points mark genes also significant in B. A positive fold change indicates the gene is upregulated in patients with LC-ILD compared with non-LC–ILD. The vertical dashed lines mark −1 and 1, and the horizontal dashed line marks P < 10−5. (D) Hallmark pathways upregulated (positive NES) and downregulated (negative NES) in patients with LC-ILD. Yellow marks significantly enriched pathways. P values from the adaptive multilevel split Monte-Carlo scheme applied in the fgsea package. (E) The top five upregulated (red) and downregulated (blue) Hallmark pathways in patients with LC-ILD. (F) Selected KEGG pathways enriched among genes upregulated in patients with LC-ILD. P values from the adaptive multilevel split Monte-Carlo scheme applied in the fgsea package. FiO2 = fraction of inspired oxygen; HRCT = high-resolution computed tomography; ILD = interstitial lung disease; KEGG = kyoto encyclopedia of genes and genomes; NES = normalized enrichment score; SaO2 = arterial saturation of oxygen.

body.p: Analysis of transcriptomic data identified 257 differentially expressed genes in LC-ILD, 230 upregulated and 27 downregulated, when compared with the combined cohort of non-LC–ILD and healthy control subjects. Among these, 25 genes remained conserved in the LC-ILD versus non-LC–ILD comparison (P < 10−5, |log2 fold change| > 1; Figures 1B and 1C and Tables E1–E3 and Excel files 1–3 in the data supplement). Upregulation of MYOM2, linked to heart function (8), suggests LC-ILD may involve systemic comorbidities beyond pulmonary disease, whereas downregulation of MALAT1 and TALAM1 implicates autophagy as a distinguishing molecular feature. Key upregulated genes included NDUFB2-AS1 and UQCRB-AS1, both of which are critical components of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation machinery. Also upregulated were NDUFA4 and COX5B (albeit not significant), subunits of respiratory chain complexes, further suggesting mitochondrial dysfunction is associated with LC-ILD.

Although less frequent than non-LC–ILD, the persistence of cough in LC-ILD (Figure 1A) may be linked to the dysregulation of connective tissue remodeling and immune pathways. Notably, COL9A2, a chondrogenic cough-associated gene, showed a trend toward downregulation across both comparisons (log2FC = −0.65, P = 0.0456; log2FC = −0.69, P = 0.0175), suggesting a potential role in structural changes to the extracellular matrix contributing to chronic cough through altered airway mechanics or fibrosis. In addition, the immune response–related gene IFNG was upregulated (log2FC = 1.33, P = 0.0817; log2FC = 1.73, P = 0.0051), although not crossing significance thresholds, suggesting persistent inflammation and immune dysregulation in patients with LC-ILD. This could potentially exacerbate symptoms such as cough and tissue remodeling and point to an interplay between fibrosis-associated remodeling and immune activation.

To determine if our findings were specific to patients with ILD or not, we incorporated publicly available data from Missailidis and colleagues (5), a geographically distinct patient cohort from Australia that used bulk RNA-sequencing data generated from peripheral blood mononuclear cells acquired from individuals with and without LC (Figure E1A). When comparing LC versus non-LC (GTEx+ COVID recovered), we found no similarities in this distinct comparison with our ILD group comparisons (Figure E1B and Excel file 7). Although there was insufficient power to detect any significantly differentially expressed genes at our set thresholds, no previously identified differentially expressed genes trended toward significance, suggesting that our findings are unique to patients with both LC and ILD. In particular, when we compared LC only versus LC-ILD, the gene HCAR3, which encodes a G-protein–coupled receptor involved in regulation of energy metabolism and fatty acid oxidation (9), crossed significance thresholds, whereas other genes trended close to significance, suggesting that a larger sample size may increase power to detect additional specific expression differences (Figure E1C and Excel file 8).

Gene set enrichment analysis on LC-ILD versus non-LC ILD differential expression results revealed significant enrichment of oxidative phosphorylation genes in LC-ILD (Figures 1D and 1E; NES = 2.78, adjusted P = 9.52 × 10−22; Table E4 and Excel files 4–6), which may be indicative of an ongoing protective response to increase ATP production as a compensatory mechanism to combat mitochondrial dysregulation underlying fatigue (4, 10–12). Furthermore, pathways involving electron transport chain activity and reactive oxygen species management were enriched, highlighting the possible role of oxidative stress in LC-ILD. Although TGF-β signaling pathways appeared to be downregulated in LC-ILD, these findings were not statistically significant. Gene set enrichment analysis using kyoto encyclopedia of genes and genomes pathway gene sets showed significant enrichment of ribosome biogenesis, oxidative phosphorylation, and Parkinson’s disease–related genes in patients with LC-ILD, suggesting an upregulation of these pathways compared with patients with non-LC–ILD (Figure 1F and Excel file 5).

Our study reveals key linked molecular pathways underlying LC-ILD may indicate mitochondrial biogenesis as a possible target for LC-ILD–associated fatigue. Still, the systemic impact of LC-ILD extends beyond pulmonary dysfunction. Overexpression of MYOM2, associated with cardiac function, suggests involvement of extrapulmonary comorbidities (8), whereas downregulation of the autophagy-related long non-coding RNA MALAT1 points to distinct cellular degradation and repair processes compared with non-LC–ILD. These observations underscore the complex, multiorgan nature of LC and its long-lasting molecular imprints, consistent with reports of systemic mitochondrial stress and chronic inflammation in post-viral syndromes. Persistent cough in pulmonary disorders may result from a convergence of dysregulated extracellular matrix remodeling, structural airway abnormalities, and cough receptor stimulation (13, 14). Downregulation of COL9A2 suggests impaired matrix integrity contributing to altered airway mechanics (13), whereas upregulation of IFNG implicates sustained IFN-driven inflammation, amplifying tissue remodeling. Together, these mechanisms place LC-ILD within the continuum of chronic fibrotic diseases, where the interplay of immune dysregulation and fibrosis perpetuates pulmonary dysfunction (15).

Limitations of this study include its single-center observational design, relatively small number of participants, reliance on physician-diagnosed LC, and limited data on vaccination histories or unrecognized acute COVID-19 episodes. The prospective matched design strengthens internal validity, although validation in larger multicenter cohorts with lung tissue analyses is essential to generalize these findings. It is yet unknown if mitochondrial dysfunction is a driver of LC-ILD, and functional data on metabolism and mitochondrial DNA quantification are needed to validate the causality of metabolic and mitochondrial dysregulation in these pathophysiologic processes.

In summary, transcriptomic profiling reveals molecular signatures distinguishing LC-ILD from non-LC–ILD and healthy states, providing actionable biomarkers for diagnosis and disease stratification. The integration of mitochondrial and fibrosis-related gene signatures into clinical workflows could inform targeted treatments aimed at halting disease progression. These findings extend beyond LC-ILD, offering insights into the systemic fibrotic and metabolic sequelae of post-viral syndromes in general, broadening relevance to other chronic inflammatory lung diseases.

sec.title: Acknowledgment
sec.p: The authors thank Yildiz Koca for her valuable feedback during the preparation of this manuscript.

fn.p: Supported by National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute grant K23HL146942 (A.A.).

Author Contributions: Conception and design (K.A.A. and A.A.). Acquisition of data for the work; analysis and interpretation; drafting the manuscript for important intellectual content; and final approval of the submitted manuscript and accountability for all aspects of the work: all authors.
This letter has a data supplement, which is accessible at the Supplements tab.
Artificial Intelligence Disclaimer: No artificial intelligence tools were used in writing this manuscript.
Author disclosures are available with the text of this letter at www.atsjournals.org.
